review_notes: Assistant title strongly indicates early-career support scope rather than independent ownership.
Explicit junior cue keeps this in the entry band.
Intern title is a direct entry-level signal.
Consultant title suggests independent role ownership without an early-career cue.
Officer title usually maps better to independent contributor work than entry support work.
No senior or executive cue and the role reads like a standard independent contributor.
Coordinator title implies operational ownership but not management hierarchy.
Officer title fits a mid-level professional IC band absent stronger leadership cues.
Head title implies supervisory leadership over a school function.
Advisor title often signals a senior specialist role even without formal line management.
Explicit senior cue keeps this in the senior band.
Manager title implies supervisory or departmental ownership short of executive tier.
Lead title indicates senior technical leadership rather than executive governance.
Officer title lacks a clear senior cue and reads closer to a mid-level professional role.
Assistant manager sits below a full manager band and is safer to review as mid-level.
Chief officer is an executive public-service appointment.
Director title indicates executive functional leadership.
Director title plus global remit places this in the executive band.
Executive here modifies kitchen rank and should not be treated as company executive leadership.
Sales executive is a lexical trap that usually refers to a quota-carrying IC role rather than executive leadership.
